Chapter 1
The Great Hall was warm and loud, a fortress against the storm hammering the enchanted ceiling. The air smelled of toasted bread and oatmeal. Remus Lupin sat at the Gryffindor table, feeling surprisingly solid. It was two weeks until the moon—that golden window where the scars were faded silver and the bone-deep ache hadn't yet returned. He felt capable. He felt hungry.

He stacked a third pancake onto his plate.

"So," Sirius Black was saying, gesturing with a piece of toast, "I told Flitwick that the collision of the two desks was a commentary on the socio-economic struggles of goblin rebellion architecture."

"He was probably crying because you singed his eyebrows, Padfoot," James Potter replied.

But James’s voice lacked its usual boom. He was hunched over his plate, his face a pale, waxy shade under the messy black hair. He wasn't eating. He was just staring at a sausage as if it were a poisonous beetle.

"I didn't singe them," Sirius argued. "They just... receded. In fear."

Peter Pettigrew giggled, spraying crumbs across the table.

Remus watched James. He looked wrong. There was a sheen of sweat on James's forehead that had nothing to do with the heat of the room.

"Prongs?" Remus asked quietly. "You alright?"

James blinked, looking up slowly. "Yeah," he said, his voice rough. He rubbed a hand over his face. "Actually... no. I've been feeling a bit under the weather. Since last night."

"You should go to Pomfrey," Remus said immediately.

"Nah," James waved a hand. He swallowed hard, his throat clicking. "Just need to... get some air."

The smell of the sausages seemed to suddenly saturate the space between them. James’s face shifted from pale to a alarming shade of green.

"James?" Sirius leaned in.

James clapped a hand over his mouth. He doubled over.

A stream of vomit exploded across the golden platters of sausages and bacon. Splatter hit the goblets and dripped onto the tablecloth. The smell of bile cut through the warm breakfast aromas like a knife.

For a second, nobody moved.

Peter let out a high-pitched squeak and scrambled backward, nearly tumbling off the bench. Sirius shot upright, his knees catching the underside of the table and jostling it, which sent a jug of pumpkin juice tipping over. Orange liquid spread toward the edge, mingling with the mess.

"Bloody hell, Prongs!" Sirius yelped, pinching his nose. "That was visceral!"

James gasped, spitting onto the mess. He looked up, eyes watering, utterly humiliated. "Told... you..."

He swayed dangerously.

"Right, that's it," Remus said, sliding out from the bench and grabbing James's arm. "Sirius, grab his other arm. Hospital Wing. Now."

"This is why I stick to toast," Sirius muttered, though he was already gripping James's shoulder, supporting the weight with surprising gentleness. "Come on, you disaster. Let's get you to bed."

"I'm fine," James mumbled weakly, wiping his mouth on his sleeve. "Just... a bug."

"Yeah, a bug that just murdered the Gryffindor breakfast," Sirius said, steering him toward the doors. "Madam Pomfrey is going to love this."

As they moved away, a sharp swish echoed behind them. Remus glanced back. Professor McGonagall had descended from the high table, her lips pressed into a thin line. With a brusque wave of her wand, she vanished the mess in an instant. A twist of her wrist replaced the soiled tablecloth with a fresh, white one, and a new platter of sausages popped into existence, steaming and innocent, as if nothing had ever happened.

Remus turned back to the cool air of the entrance hall. It wasn't fatal, but it was messy enough to ensure James wouldn't be leaving the Hospital Wing for a week. Madame Pomfrey didn't mess around with stomach bugs.

Remus felt a strange, sharp pang of guilt. James was miserable. But in the back of his mind, a quiet thought took root: He's gone.

Sirius would be pacing the common room within the hour, climbing the walls with boredom and worry. He would need a distraction. And Remus, for the first time in weeks, felt strong enough to be one.

The Hospital Wing smelled of liniment and starch. Madam Pomfrey took one look at James’s greenish pallor and ushered him into a curtained bed with the efficiency of a field medic.

"Stomach flu," she diagnosed, waving her wand over a glass of potion that turned a violent shade of purple. "Drink this. And don't let him leave that bed for three days, minimum."

James swallowed the potion with a grimace and collapsed back against the pillows, looking pathetic.

"Try not to die, Prongs," Sirius said, backing away as Pomfrey shooed them toward the door. "We'll bring you homework."

"Don't you dare," James mumbled, already pulling the sheets up.

They sprinted down the marble staircase, taking two steps at a time. The corridors were emptying; the bell was due to ring any second.

They slid through the door of the Transfiguration classroom just as the clock on the wall began to chime. Professor McGonagall was writing on the blackboard, her back straight as a rod. She didn't turn around, but her chalk paused for a fraction of a second as they tiptoed to their usual seats in the back row.

Remus sat down, breathless. A second later, Sirius dropped into the chair beside him.

The chair on the other side of Sirius remained empty. James’s usually chaotic desk was clear—no half-finished essay, no doodles of Snitches, no stack of Chocolate Frog cards.

McGonagall turned around, her spectacles glinting. She scanned the room, her gaze flickering over the empty seat.

"Mr. Black," she said. "I see Mr. Potter has decided to spare my classroom the contents of his stomach."

She turned back to the board, tapping it with her wand. The words HUMAN TO ANIMAGUS: THE PHYSIOLOGY OF TRANSFORMATION appeared in elegant, swirling script.

"Open your books to page 394," she commanded.

The rest of the day seemed to drag. Without James to whisper jokes across the History of Magic aisle or pass illicit notes during Charms, the castle felt strangely large and echoing. The other students went about their business, but the gap at the Gryffindor table was impossible to ignore.

By the time the final bell rang, the gray light of afternoon was already fading into a dreary twilight. Remus and Sirius walked back to the tower in a comfortable silence, though Sirius kept tapping his wand against his leg, a rhythmic, impatient beat.

The common room was a welcome relief of warmth and noise. The fire was roaring in the grate, casting flickering orange shadows over the worn armchairs. It was crowded, students sprawled on the rug finishing essays or huddled in corners gossiping.

Remus claimed his usual seat near the fire, sinking into the worn velvet. He opened The Dark Forces: A Guide to Self-Protection, grateful for the distraction. The words on the page blurred slightly—he was tired, but it was a normal tiredness, not the bone-crushing exhaustion of the week before.

Sirius dropped into the chair opposite him, throwing his legs over the armrest. He pulled out a roll of parchment and a quill, but instead of writing, he spent ten minutes trying to see if he could balance the inkwell on the end of his nose.

"It's going to tip," Remus said, not looking up from his chapter on counter-curses. "And you're going to stain the rug, and Filch will make you scrub it with a toothbrush."

"You have no sense of adventure, Moony," Sirius mumbled, the inkwell wobbling precariously. "It's about balance. Focus. Inner peace."

"It's about making a mess."

The portrait hole swung open with a creak, letting in a draft of cooler air. Peter Pettigrew scrambled through, looking flushed and breathless. He craned his neck, spotted them by the fire, and hurried over, dodging a pair of first-years playing Wizard's Chess.

"Well?" Sirius sat up so fast the inkwell slid off his nose and landed safely in his lap with a soft thud. "Is he still alive? Did he survive the Great Sausage Massacre?"

Peter collapsed onto the rug between their chairs, wiping sweat from his forehead. "He’s asleep. Pomfrey gave him a Sleeping Draught."

"Poor Prongs," Sirius said, though a grin was tugging at the corner of his mouth. "Finally, some peace and quiet."

"He looks awful," Peter added, pulling a squashed bag of Bertie Bott's Every Flavor Beans from his pocket. "Pale as a sheet. She says he's severely dehydrated. She wouldn't let me stay—she said he needs to rest. He won't be up for dinner, and she said she’s keeping him overnight. Maybe two days."

"Two days?" Sirius raised his eyebrows, tossing a bean into his mouth and grimacing—boiled cabbage flavor. "For a stomach bug? She just likes the drama of it all."

"She said he needs to be monitored," Peter mumbled, chewing rapidly. "She wasn't happy when I tried to wake him up to say hi. She practically shoved me out the door."

Dinner had been uneventful. The food was bland, the conversation sparse, and they finished quickly. They headed up to the dormitory to change.

The room was filled with the usual sounds of the evening—buckles clinking, robes hitting the floor. Sirius was stripping off his clothes with aggressive, jerky movements. Peter had turned his back to the room, changing with frantic modesty.

Sirius turned, spotted him, and let out a short, sharp, barking laugh.

"Oi, Wormtail," Sirius crowed, leaning against his bedpost with a predatory grin. "Where's your knob? Did you forget to do that bit last time you transformed back into a human?"

Remus flinched. "Keep your voice down," he hissed, crossing the room. "Do you want the whole school to hear?"

"Hear what Moony? About Peter's teeny weenie? It's not news mate."

Peter flushed a deep, miserable red, snatching his pajama bottoms and yanking them up with trembling hands. "Oh, very funny," he muttered, his voice high and tight. "I haven't heard that one a thousand times."

He thrust his hand up, extending two fingers in a rude gesture right under Sirius's nose. Then, without waiting for a reaction, he turned on his heel and marched to his bed, yanking the curtains shut with a violent snap.

Remus turned toward his own four-poster and reached for the hangings, but Sirius's voice cut through the din behind him.

"Moony." He sat cross-legged on his mattress, the battered chessboard open in front of him. "James is sick. Fancy sitting in? I might actually win one tonight."

Remus came back and settled at the foot of the bed. "I'm not very good."

"I know," Sirius said with a laugh. "Pick a color."

"Black, I suppose."

"Excellent. Pawn to E4." The white stone soldier marched forward and stopped on its square.

"Pawn to E5." The black pawn stepped out to meet it, a mirror of aggression, and Sirius drummed his fingers against his knee in a restless rhythm that matched the game. "Slow tonight."

"It's been a long day."

"Dull, you mean." Sirius leaned forward, chin in hand. "Knight to F3." The white knight trotted out with a heavy clatter, carving its path through the ranks. The conversation wandered along with the pieces—the sausages James had thrown up over at breakfast, the heroic manner in which he'd done it, the relative merits of peace versus noise—and Remus answered in the half-distracted way of a man losing slowly and not minding much, until Sirius looked up from the board and didn't look back down again.

"Whatever happened with you and Cordelia Flint?" he asked. "Before she ran off to Marcus Avery. I was sure you were in there, mate, and then—gone. Was she a good snog at least?"

The heat crawled up Remus's neck. "Sirius—"

"How far did you get?"

"Not far." He kept his eyes on the polished wood of the bedframe. "I got my hand up her shirt. That was it."

"That was it?" Sirius's eyebrows climbed. "She didn't—"

"She pulled away. She saw the scars. All the ones down my side." He gestured vaguely at his own face, his voice dropping. "It's hard enough talking to girls without looking like I'm about to fall over. I'm peaky three weeks out of four. Hardly an attraction."

The mockery went out of Sirius's expression. "That's the whole catalogue, then. One hand. One shirt."

"Keep your voice down."

"Bishop takes pawn." The white bishop strode forward and cracked its crosier against the black pawn, sweeping the wreckage to the edge of the board. "That's genuinely tragic, Moony, and I'm not being dramatic."

"You're always being dramatic."

"Not about this. This I'm horrified about, properly, on your behalf." He pretended to study the board while his fingers kept up their restless drumming. "You're how old and you've only ever had one tit in your hand. That's the entire history?"

"Some of us have other things to worry about. Such as not eating people once a month."

"Make it a joke so we stop talking about it." Sirius slid his rook out. "Rook to E1. I'm serious."

"You're Sirius, lovely to—"

"I will smother you with this pillow."

Remus moved a knight to nowhere in particular, and Sirius let the game drift, leaning back against the headboard with his eyes off the board entirely.

"Fawley," he said.

"What about her?"

"Caroline Fawley. Last Hogsmeade weekend." He said it the way he said everything that cost him nothing. "Three Broomsticks first, then the Hog's Head after. There's a back room. She used her mouth."

Remus's hand stalled over his queen. "Did she."

"That's my whole point, see." Sirius sat forward, and the grey eyes sharpened. "I have been touched by another human being. Hands, mouths, the lot. And I'm telling you it is not the same. It isn't remotely the same as doing it yourself. It's like—" He cast about for it. "You know how chocolate tastes after a transformation? After you've been torn apart and put back together and Pomfrey hands you that first square."

Remus said nothing. He knew exactly how it tasted.

"Like that. But everywhere, all at once." Sirius turned a captured pawn between his fingers. "When she went down I thought I'd pass out. Not because she was any good—God love her, she caught me with her teeth twice and I nearly went through the ceiling—but because it was somebody else. Someone else's warmth. Someone else's timing, that you can't predict. You can't do that with your own hand, Moony. Your cock knows what your hand's about to do. There's no surprise in it."

"Bishop to B4." The piece settled with a faintly disapproving look.

Peter probably does—Mary MacDonald's been giving him a very strange look lately. You don't. And it's not because you're ugly, or boring, or whatever miserable thing you've decided about yourself. It's because one girl got spooked and you built a wall around the whole subject and moved in behind it."

Remus prodded a pawn forward, harder than the move required. "Pawn to C6."

"Defense," Sirius said. "Always defense."

The fire downstairs must have been stoked; warmth pushed up through the floorboards. Peter's snoring had thickened into a steady whistling drone two beds over.

"The point is you're missing something." Sirius's voice had dropped now, gone quieter and rougher. "Not technique. Not practice. Just the feeling. What it's like when the hand isn't yours. And because of one bad afternoon you've decided to opt out. For good, apparently."

"Not for good. Just until—" Remus stopped.

"Until what? Until the scars heal? They don't, mate, that's rather the nature of them." Sirius gestured at him with the captured pawn. "You could wait the rest of your life. You shut your eyes and you find out what it feels like when the grip isn't your own, and then it's done and we finish the game."

The laugh died. The quiet came back. Remus looked down at the board, at his king standing alone in a field of wreckage, one move from checkmate. His hand found the hangings. He could pull them open, swing his legs off the bed, cross to his own four-poster, and in the dark let the whole evening dissolve into something they never spoke of again.
